ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR (Carbonated soft drinks) is the closest defensible category for a yuzu-flavoured carbonated beverage; no yuzu-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for yuzu soda; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass serving of iced yuzu soda is approximately 350 g, equivalent to a typical restaurant beverage portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Carbonated beverage, lemon-lime (SR Legacy ID 14148)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo entry exists for yuzu soda. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry 'Carbonated beverage, lemon-lime' (FDC ID 14148), which is the closest approved-source compositional analogue: energy ~38–41 kcal/100g, carbohydrate ~9.9–10.1g/100g, sugar ~9.0–9.2g/100g, sodium ~3mg/100g, protein ~0.1g/100g, fat 0g/100g. The input grabfood values (energy 40 kcal, carbohydrate 10g, sugar 9.14g, sodium 2.86mg, protein 0.14g, fat 0g) are consistent with this USDA reference and are retained. Vitamin C (4.29mg/100g) is consistent with yuzu juice dilution in a carbonated beverage and is supported by USDA FoodData Central data for citrus juice components. Fat is confirmed zero for a carbonated sugar-water beverage by product formulation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); GI values cross-referenced with University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yuzu soda. The University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) report GI values of approximately 59–65 for sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drinks (cola, lemon-lime, and similar SSBs). A GI of 63 is used as the central analogous estimate for this sugar-sweetened carbonated yuzu beverage (9.14g sugar/100g, predominantly sucrose/fructose from added sugar and yuzu syrup). Glycemic load per 350g serving = (63/100) × (10.0g carbohydrate × 350g/100g) = (63/100) × 35.0 = 22.05, rounded to 22.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (9.14g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure risk independently of dietary sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage; the 9.14g/100g added sugar exceeds the AHA 2018 SSB threshold and independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite the very low sodium content of 2.86mg/100g — request unsweetened yuzu sparkling water instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.14g sugar/100g (predominantly added sugar), zero dietary fibre, and negligible protein — liquid sugar causes rapid glucose absorption and acute postprandial glycaemic excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; as a sugar-sweetened liquid with 9.14g sugar/100g, zero fibre, and a glycaemic load of 22.1 per 350g serving, it causes rapid glucose absorption and is classified as unsuitable per ADA sugar-sweetened beverage guidelines — unsweetened yuzu sparkling water is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk via dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and systemic inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage; the 9.14g/100g added sugar (31.99g per 350g serving) is associated with elevated triglycerides, visceral fat accumulation, and systemic inflammation per AHA dietary guidelines — occasional consumption is lower risk than regular intake, but unsweetened alternatives are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Yuzu Soda is a chilled, carbonated soft drink flavoured with yuzu, a fragrant East Asian citrus fruit (Citrus junos) prized for its tart, floral, and aromatic profile that combines notes of grapefruit, mandarin, and lemon. In Singapore's Japanese restaurant context, it is typically prepared by mixing yuzu juice or yuzu syrup with carbonated water and serving over ice in a tall glass. The beverage is refreshing and visually appealing, with a pale yellow hue and a bright citrus aroma. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 40 kcal per 100g, with 10g carbohydrate and 9.14g sugar per 100g — almost entirely from added sugar — and negligible protein (0.14g/100g) and zero fat. A standard 350g glass delivers approximately 140 kcal and 35g carbohydrate. Vitamin C is present at 4.29mg/100g from the yuzu juice component. Yuzu contains beneficial flavonoids including hesperidin and naringenin with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are substantially offset by the high added sugar content in the commercial preparation. Clinically, this is a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Dietary fibre is absent, meaning there is no modulation of glucose absorption. The beverage is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in yuzu soda?
Is yuzu soda suitable for diabetics?
How much sugar is in yuzu soda?
What is the glycemic index of yuzu soda?
Can I drink yuzu soda if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between yuzu juice and yuzu soda?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo entry exists for yuzu soda. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy lemon-lime carbonated soft drink (FDC ID 14148), which is the closest approved-source compositional analogue. The input grabfood values are consistent with this reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables for sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drinks. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a carbonated sugar-water beverage with no fat-containing ingredients. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by formulation. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — carbonated soft drinks contain no dietary fibre. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific yuzu soda preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy FDC ID 14148) or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. Vitamin C (4.29mg/100g) is retained from the input as consistent with yuzu juice dilution in a carbonated beverage and supported by USDA FoodData Central citrus juice composition data. The dish name '956D Iced Summer Yuzu Soda(D)' contains a menu code prefix (956D) and suffix (D) that are restaurant-specific identifiers; the canonical food name is normalised to 'Iced Yuzu Soda'. This beverage is served in a Japanese restaurant context in Singapore but yuzu soda is a Japanese-origin beverage; primary cuisine is classified as Japanese. Glycaemic load per 350g serving = (63/100) × 35.0g available carbohydrate = 22.1. Nutrient source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 14148 (lemon-lime carbonated beverage) used as closest approved-source analogue; grabfood values are consistent with this reference and retained. GI source: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283, cross-referenced with University of Sydney GI Database.

Iteration 2: Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent'. Updated nutrient_source URL to direct USDA FoodData Central food-details link.

Fixed Q4 GI source compliance — updated gi_source to cite Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables (Diabetes Care journal) cross-referenced with University of Sydney GI Database, both of which are approved GI sources per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. Removed any reference to non-approved sources. Expanded notes to document all -1 fields and zero-macro justifications explicitly.
